questions.question: Match each CNN component to what it does.
categories: Slides over the input and computes weighted sums to detect patterns
The output produced by applying one kernel to the input
Reduces spatial dimensions by summarizing local regions
Takes the flattened features and produces final class predictions

correct_mapping.Kernel/Filter: Slides over the input and computes weighted sums to detect patterns
correct_mapping.Feature map: The output produced by applying one kernel to the input
correct_mapping.Pooling layer: Reduces spatial dimensions by summarizing local regions
correct_mapping.Fully connected layer: Takes the flattened features and produces final class predictions

questions.explanation: A kernel is a small set of learnable weights that detects patterns like edges. Each kernel produces a feature map showing where the pattern appears. Pooling shrinks the spatial size by keeping only summary statistics (e.g., the max). Finally, fully connected layers use the flattened features for classification.

questions.question: Match each convolution parameter to its effect on the output.
categories: Produces a smaller output by skipping positions
Helps control output size by adding zeros around the input
Increases the number of output feature maps (channels)
Each kernel covers a larger area of the input at each position

correct_mapping.Larger stride: Produces a smaller output by skipping positions
correct_mapping.Adding padding: Helps control output size by adding zeros around the input
correct_mapping.More kernels: Increases the number of output feature maps (channels)
correct_mapping.Larger kernel size: Each kernel covers a larger area of the input at each position

questions.explanation: Stride determines how far the kernel moves between positions; larger stride means fewer positions and smaller output. Padding adds zeros around the border to prevent shrinking. The number of kernels determines how many feature maps (channels) the layer outputs. A larger kernel covers more of the input at once.

questions.question: Match each type of feature to where it is typically detected in a CNN.
categories: Early/first convolutional layers
Second and third layers
Middle layers
Deepest layers
questions.explanation: CNNs learn a hierarchy of features. The first layers detect simple edges and lines. The next layers combine edges into textures and corners. Middle layers compose textures into recognizable parts like eyes or wheels. The deepest layers represent entire objects by combining parts together.

questions.question: Match each CNN property to its description.
categories: The same kernel weights are used at every spatial position
Shifting the input shifts the feature map the same way
Each output value depends only on a small local region of the input
Simple features combine into increasingly complex ones through layers

correct_mapping.Parameter sharing: The same kernel weights are used at every spatial position
correct_mapping.Translation equivariance: Shifting the input shifts the feature map the same way
correct_mapping.Local connectivity: Each output value depends only on a small local region of the input
correct_mapping.Hierarchical features: Simple features combine into increasingly complex ones through layers

questions.explanation: Parameter sharing reuses the same weights everywhere, drastically reducing the number of parameters. This sharing causes translation equivariance, so patterns are detected regardless of position. Local connectivity means each output only looks at a small patch. Stacking layers builds a hierarchy from simple edges to complex objects.

questions.question: Match each kernel type to what it detects.
categories: Responds to changes between top and bottom rows
Responds to changes between left and right columns
Responds to changes along a diagonal direction
Responds to repeating small-scale patterns

correct_mapping.Horizontal edge kernel: Responds to changes between top and bottom rows
correct_mapping.Vertical edge kernel: Responds to changes between left and right columns
correct_mapping.Diagonal edge kernel: Responds to changes along a diagonal direction

questions.explanation: Different kernels are sensitive to different patterns. A horizontal edge kernel has contrasting values in its top and bottom rows, so it fires at horizontal boundaries. A vertical edge kernel has contrasting left and right columns. Diagonal kernels detect edges at angles. Texture kernels respond to repetitive local patterns.

questions.question: Match each pooling concept to its description.
categories: Keeps only the largest value in each region
Computes the mean of all values in each region
Determines how large each pooling region is
Controls how far the pooling window moves between regions

correct_mapping.Max pooling: Keeps only the largest value in each region
correct_mapping.Average pooling: Computes the mean of all values in each region
correct_mapping.Pooling window size: Determines how large each pooling region is
correct_mapping.Pooling stride: Controls how far the pooling window moves between regions

questions.explanation: Max pooling retains the strongest activation in each window, emphasizing the presence of features. Average pooling takes the mean, preserving overall activation levels. The window size (e.g., 2x2) sets the area being summarized, and the stride (e.g., 2) determines how much the window moves, controlling how much the output shrinks.

questions.question: Match each stage of a typical CNN architecture to its role.
categories: Extract spatial features using learned kernels
Reduce spatial dimensions while retaining important information
Convert 3D feature maps into a 1D vector
Combine all features to make a final prediction

correct_mapping.Convolutional layers: Extract spatial features using learned kernels
correct_mapping.Pooling layers: Reduce spatial dimensions while retaining important information
correct_mapping.Flatten operation: Convert 3D feature maps into a 1D vector
correct_mapping.Fully connected layers: Combine all features to make a final prediction

questions.explanation: A CNN pipeline starts with convolutional layers that detect features at various levels of complexity. Pooling layers reduce the spatial size, making computation manageable. The flatten step reshapes the 3D feature maps into a 1D vector. Fully connected layers then use all the extracted features to produce class predictions.

questions.question: Match each term related to channels to its meaning.
categories: 3 channels representing Red, Green, and Blue
Number of feature maps produced by a convolutional layer
The kernel spans all input channels
Changes the number of channels without changing spatial size

correct_mapping.Input channels (RGB image): 3 channels representing Red, Green, and Blue
correct_mapping.Output channels: Number of feature maps produced by a convolutional layer
correct_mapping.Kernel depth: The kernel spans all input channels
correct_mapping.1x1 convolution: Changes the number of channels without changing spatial size

questions.explanation: An RGB image has 3 input channels. Each kernel in a conv layer spans all input channels (its depth matches the input depth) and produces one output feature map. The number of kernels determines the number of output channels. A 1x1 convolution mixes across channels at each position to adjust the channel count.

questions.question: Match each output size factor to how it affects the convolution output.
categories: Larger input produces larger output
Larger kernel causes the output to shrink more
Adding more padding increases the output size
Larger stride decreases the output size

correct_mapping.Kernel size: Larger kernel causes the output to shrink more
correct_mapping.Padding: Adding more padding increases the output size
correct_mapping.Stride: Larger stride decreases the output size

questions.explanation: The output size formula is (input - kernel + 2*padding) / stride + 1. A larger input gives more positions for the kernel. A larger kernel means fewer valid positions (output shrinks). More padding expands the effective input, counteracting shrinkage. A larger stride means the kernel skips more positions, reducing the output.

questions.question: Match each advantage of CNNs over fully connected networks for images to its explanation.
categories: Parameter sharing means the same kernel weights are reused everywhere
Kernels preserve the 2D structure of the input instead of flattening it
A pattern detected in one location is detected the same way elsewhere
Can handle large images without an explosion in the number of weights

correct_mapping.Fewer parameters: Parameter sharing means the same kernel weights are reused everywhere
correct_mapping.Spatial awareness: Kernels preserve the 2D structure of the input instead of flattening it
correct_mapping.Translation equivariance: A pattern detected in one location is detected the same way elsewhere
correct_mapping.Scalability: Can handle large images without an explosion in the number of weights

questions.explanation: CNNs use parameter sharing so a small kernel is reused across the entire input, keeping the parameter count low. Unlike fully connected layers that flatten the image, convolutions operate on the 2D spatial structure. Because the same kernel applies everywhere, features are detected regardless of position. This all means CNNs scale well to large images.
